title: The Sanction of the Victim: Why Builders Keep Apologizing

source.original: Atlas Shrugged — Ayn Rand / The Genealogy of Morality — Friedrich Nietzsche

body_html:
The most radical idea in Atlas Shrugged isn't the strike. It isn't the motor. It's the concept of the sanction of the victim — the idea that oppression only works when the oppressed agree that they deserve it.

Galt's key insight isn't that the looters are powerful. It's that they're powerless without the producers' cooperation. James Taggart can't run a railroad. Wesley Mouch can't build a bridge. The entire parasitic system depends on one thing: that Dagny, Rearden, and the other creators keep showing up, keep producing, keep accepting guilt for their own excellence. The machine runs on misplaced shame.

Nietzsche identified the same mechanism in the Genealogy of Morality: the strong adopt the weak's moral framework and begin judging themselves by it. The noble who once said "I am good, therefore what I do is good" starts saying "Am I good? Let me check with the priest." The shift is catastrophic. Once you accept that your strength requires justification — that producing, building, excelling needs permission — you've handed the keys to people who will never use them.

Watch tech founders in 2026. They build products used by billions, create thousands of jobs, generate massive value — and they spend half their public appearances apologizing. Apologizing for being too big, too profitable, too successful, too fast. Congressional hearings where senators who've never built anything grill CEOs who've built everything. The CEOs sit there and take it. They've sanctioned the premise.

The crypto world has its own version. DeFi builders who created genuinely revolutionary financial infrastructure — permissionless lending, decentralized exchanges, programmable money — spent years saying "we want to work with regulators" to regulators who wanted them dead. The CFTC's "regulation by enforcement" era worked not because the CFTC was powerful but because builders accepted the framework that said they needed permission to innovate.

Rearden's trial is the turning point. He refuses to defend himself — not because he's guilty but because the court has no legitimate authority to judge production. He withdraws his sanction. "If you believe you have the right to force me — use your guns. I will not help you pretend this is justice." The system panics. It literally cannot function without the victim's cooperation.

Nietzsche's Übermensch doesn't withdraw from society because he hates it. He withdraws because he refuses to evaluate himself by the Last Man's rubric. The blink is contagious — but only if you flinch. The moment a builder says "I don't owe you an apology for being good at what I do," the entire ressentiment architecture collapses.

The sanction of the victim is always voluntary. Which means it can always be revoked. Rand's entire novel is built on the question: what if they just... stopped apologizing?
